does: This paper proposes Reward-Weighted Sampling (RWS), a decoding strategy for masked diffusion language models that uses an external reward model to guide token selection during the iterative diffusion process. Rather than selecting tokens independently based on individual confidence scores at each step—which inadvertently produces autoregressive-like generation orders—RWS evaluates the quality of entire intermediate sequences and rescales token logits accordingly. This approach selectively boosts confidence for lower-scoring tokens, promoting more genuinely non-autoregressive generation patterns that exploit the parallel decoding capabilities of masked diffusion models. The method integrates global sequence-level coherence signals into the token selection mechanism across diffusion steps.
connects: This work extends the recent line of masked diffusion models for language generation, which emerged as non-autoregressive alternatives to standard left-to-right autoregressive transformers. The paper builds on prior research in diffusion-based language modeling and non-autoregressive decoding strategies, addressing a fundamental limitation observed in naive confidence-based sampling for masked diffusion models. The approach connects to reinforcement learning from human feedback and reward modeling literature by leveraging external reward signals to guide generation, similar to how such signals have enhanced other generation paradigms. The work also relates to broader research on iterative refinement and parallel decoding in sequence generation, seeking to preserve the computational benefits of non-autoregressive approaches while improving output quality through better decoding heuristics.
enables: This work points toward improved decoding strategies for masked diffusion language models that can more effectively leverage their non-autoregressive nature. It enables future research on integrating global constraints and evaluation signals into diffusion-based generation, potentially inspiring hybrid approaches combining local token-level information with sequence-level guidance. The method could be extended to incorporate task-specific rewards or human preferences more directly into the decoding process, opening paths toward controllable generation in masked diffusion models. Additionally, this work motivates investigation of when and how global signals should override local confidence estimates, potentially leading to more sophisticated annealing schedules or adaptive weighting schemes across diffusion steps. The approach could eventually inform the design of masked diffusion models that naturally produce non-autoregressive generation orders without requiring post-hoc decoding interventions.
